title: Acute Epiglottitis
chief_complaint: Severe sore throat, difficulty swallowing, and I feel like something is stuck in my throat. I'm sitting forward and drooling because I can't swallow. My voice is muffled. It started yesterday and has gotten rapidly worse.

physical_exam.General: Toxic-appearing. Sitting upright in tripod position (leaning forward, hands on knees). Drooling. Stridor (inspiratory). Muffled voice.
physical_exam.HEENT: DO NOT EXAMINE THROAT! (may precipitate complete airway obstruction). No trismus (differentiates from PTA).

hidden_data.lateral_neck_xr: Thumb sign: Enlarged, rounded epiglottis (thumbprint sign). Thickened aryepiglottic folds. Normal subglottic airway.
hidden_data.fiberoptic: Epiglottis: Cherry-red, swollen, edematous. Aryepiglottic folds edematous. Vocal cords normal. Airway narrowed to 50%. Performed in OR with anesthesia prepared for emergency surgical airway.

correct_diagnosis: Acute Epiglottitis (Supraglottitis) from Haemophilus influenzae type B - IMPENDING AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION
key_learning_points: Epiglottitis = inflammation of epiglottis and supraglottic structures. TRUE AIRWAY EMERGENCY. Since Hib vaccine: Incidence decreased 99% in children, now more common in adults (age 40-60). Other causes: Streptococcus pneumoniae, GAS, Staphylococcus, viral, thermal injury

Classic triad: Sore throat + Odynophagia + Respiratory distress. Tripod position (maximizes airway diameter). Drooling (cannot swallow). Muffled 'hot potato' voice. Stridor (LATE sign - indicates >50% airway obstruction). Rapid progression over hours

DO NOT examine throat, attempt tongue blade exam, place IV, or obtain ABG in uncooperative or distressed patient. These can precipitate complete airway obstruction. Keep patient calm, with parent/caregiver. Allow position of comfort

Diagnosis: Lateral neck XR (thumb sign - enlarged epiglottis). Fiberoptic laryngoscopy (gold standard) - MUST be done in controlled setting (OR or ICU) with anesthesia/ENT prepared for emergency airway. CT if abscess suspected

Treatment: SECURE AIRWAY FIRST. Intubation in OR with fiberoptic scope by most experienced provider (ENT/Anesthesia). Prepare for difficult airway: Smaller ETT than predicted (0.5-1mm smaller), surgical airway equipment available (cricothyroidotomy, tracheostomy). Extubate after 24-48h of antibiotics when leak around ETT present

Antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 2g IV q24h + Vancomycin (cover MRSA) + Metronidazole. Steroids: Dexamethasone 10mg IV (reduces edema). ICU admission. Household contacts: Rifampin prophylaxis if unvaccinated <4 years or immunocompromised contacts
